| * [v](/wiki/Template:Political_divisions_of_the_United_States "Template:Political divisions of the United States") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Political_divisions_of_the_United_States "Template talk:Political divisions of the United States") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Political_divisions_of_the_United_States "Special:EditPage/Template:Political divisions of the United States")

(**CDP**)[[1]](#cite_note-USCB-1)[[2]](#cite_note-GARM_Chapter_9_-_Places-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Federal_Register-3) is a [concentration of population](/wiki/Place_(United_States_Census_Bureau) "Place (United States Census Bureau)") defined by the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") for statistical purposes only. The U.S. Census Bureau defines a CDP as a "statistical geography representing closely settled, unincorporated communities that are locally recognized and identified by name."[[4]](#cite_note-CDPCriteria-4)

| * [v](/wiki/Template:Political_divisions_of_the_United_States "Template:Political divisions of the United States") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Political_divisions_of_the_United_States "Template talk:Political divisions of the United States") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Political_divisions_of_the_United_States "Special:EditPage/Template:Political divisions of the United States") |

A **census-designated place** (**CDP**)[[1]](#cite_note-USCB-1)[[2]](#cite_note-GARM_Chapter_9_-_Places-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Federal_Register-3) is a [concentration of population](/wiki/Place_(United_States_Census_Bureau) "Place (United States Census Bureau)") defined by the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") for statistical purposes only. The U.S. Census Bureau defines a CDP as a "statistical geography representing closely settled, unincorporated communities that are locally recognized and identified by name."[[4]](#cite_note-CDPCriteria-4)

CDPs have been used in each decennial census since 1980 as the counterparts of [incorporated places](/wiki/Place_(United_States_Census_Bureau)#Incorporated_place "Place (United States Census Bureau)"),[[5]](#cite_note-5) such as self-governing [cities](/wiki/Local_government_in_the_United_States#Municipal_governments "Local government in the United States"), [towns](/wiki/Town#United_States "Town"), and [villages](/wiki/Village_(United_States) "Village (United States)"), for the purposes of gathering and correlating statistical data. CDPs are populated areas that generally include one officially designated but currently [unincorporated](/wiki/Unincorporated_area "Unincorporated area") community, for which the CDP is named, and may include surrounding inhabited countryside of varying dimensions and, occasionally, other, smaller unincorporated communities. CDPs include small rural communities, [edge cities](/wiki/Edge_city "Edge city"), [colonias](/wiki/Colonia_(United_States) "Colonia (United States)") located along the [Mexico–United States border](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border "Mexico–United States border"), and unincorporated [resorts](/wiki/Resort "Resort") and [retirement communities](/wiki/Retirement_community "Retirement community") and their environs. The boundaries of any CDP may change from decade to decade, and the Census Bureau may de-establish a CDP after a period of study, then re-establish it some decades later. Most unincorporated areas within the United States are not and have not been included in any CDP.[[6]](#cite_note-Census2010-6)

The boundaries of a CDP have no legal status[[1]](#cite_note-USCB-1) and may not always correspond with the local understanding of the area or community with the same name.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] However, criteria established for the [2010 census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census") require that a CDP name "be one that is recognized and used in daily communication by the residents of the community" (not "a name developed solely for planning or other purposes") and recommend that a CDP's boundaries be mapped based on the geographic extent associated with inhabitants' regular use of the named place. There is no provision, however, that this name recognition be unanimous for all residents, or that all residents use the community for which the CDP is named for services provided therein. There is no mandatory correlation between CDP names or boundaries and those established for other human purposes, such as post office names or zones, political precincts, or school districts.[[6]](#cite_note-Census2010-6)

Although census-designated places are not considered incorporated places, the Census Bureau includes CDPs in its city population list for [Hawaii](/wiki/Hawaii "Hawaii") because that state has no incorporated cities.[[7]](#cite_note-PopEstBigDens-7) In addition, census city lists from 2007 included [Arlington County, Virginia](/wiki/Arlington_County,_Virginia "Arlington County, Virginia")'s CDP in the list with the incorporated places,[[8]](#cite_note-8) but since 2010, only the Urban Honolulu CDP, Hawaii, representing the historic core of [Honolulu, Hawaii](/wiki/Honolulu "Honolulu"), is shown in the city and town estimates.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census-designated_place&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The Census Bureau reported data for some unincorporated places as early as the [first census in 1790](/wiki/1790_United_States_census "1790 United States census") (for example, [Louisville, Kentucky](/wiki/Louisville,_Kentucky "Louisville, Kentucky"), which was not legally incorporated in Kentucky until 1828), though usage continued to develop through the [1890 census](/wiki/1890_United_States_census "1890 United States census"), in which the census mixed unincorporated places with incorporated places in its products with "town" or "village" as its label.[[2]](#cite_note-GARM_Chapter_9_-_Places-2) This made it confusing to determine which of the "towns" were or were not incorporated.[[2]](#cite_note-GARM_Chapter_9_-_Places-2)

The [1900](/wiki/1900_United_States_census "1900 United States census") through [1930 censuses](/wiki/1930_United_States_census "1930 United States census") did not report data for unincorporated places.[[2]](#cite_note-GARM_Chapter_9_-_Places-2)

For the [1940 census](/wiki/1940_United_States_census "1940 United States census"), the Census Bureau compiled a separate report of unofficial, unincorporated communities of 500 or more people.[[2]](#cite_note-GARM_Chapter_9_-_Places-2) The Census Bureau officially defined this category as "unincorporated places" in the [1950 census](/wiki/1950_United_States_census "1950 United States census") and used that term through the 1970 census.[[2]](#cite_note-GARM_Chapter_9_-_Places-2) For the 1950 census, these types of places were identified only outside "[urbanized areas](/wiki/List_of_United_States_urban_areas "List of United States urban areas")".[[2]](#cite_note-GARM_Chapter_9_-_Places-2) In [1960](/wiki/1960_United_States_census "1960 United States census"), the Census Bureau also identified unincorporated places inside urbanized areas (except in [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England"), whose political geography is based on the [New England town](/wiki/New_England_town "New England town"), and is distinctly different from other areas of the U.S.), but with a population of at least 10,000.[[2]](#cite_note-GARM_Chapter_9_-_Places-2) For the [1970 census](/wiki/1970_United_States_census "1970 United States census"), the population threshold for "unincorporated places" in urbanized areas was reduced to 5,000.[[2]](#cite_note-GARM_Chapter_9_-_Places-2)

For the [1980 census](/wiki/1980_United_States_census "1980 United States census"), the designation was changed to "census designated places"[[2]](#cite_note-GARM_Chapter_9_-_Places-2) and the designation was made available for places inside urbanized areas in New England.[[2]](#cite_note-GARM_Chapter_9_-_Places-2) For the [1990 census](/wiki/1990_United_States_census "1990 United States census"), the population threshold for CDPs in urbanized areas was reduced to 2,500.[[2]](#cite_note-GARM_Chapter_9_-_Places-2) From 1950 through 1990, the Census Bureau specified other population requirements for unincorporated places or CDPs in [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska"), [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico"), island areas, and [Native American reservations](/wiki/Indian_reservation "Indian reservation"). Minimum population criteria for CDPs were dropped with the [2000 census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census").[[3]](#cite_note-Federal_Register-3)[[6]](#cite_note-Census2010-6)

The Census Bureau's Participant Statistical Areas Program (PSAP) allows designated participants to propose new CDPS as well as review and suggest modifications to the boundaries of existing CDPs.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The PSAP was to be offered to county and municipal planning agencies in 2008.

## Effects of designation and examples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census-designated_place&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Effects of designation and examples")]

The boundaries of such places are typically defined in cooperation with local county or tribal officials, but are not fixed, and do not affect the status of local government or incorporation; the territories thus defined are strictly statistical entities. CDP boundaries may change from one census to the next to reflect changes in settlement patterns.[[1]](#cite_note-USCB-1)[[2]](#cite_note-GARM_Chapter_9_-_Places-2) Further, as statistical entities, the boundaries of the CDP may not correspond with local understanding of the area with the same name. Recognized communities may be divided into two or more CDPs while on the other hand, two or more communities may be combined into one CDP. A CDP may also cover the unincorporated part of a named community, where the rest lies within an incorporated place.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

CDPs are treated in the same way as incorporated entities and appear in the same category of census data as incorporated places. This distinguishes CDPs from other census classifications, such as [minor civil divisions](/wiki/Minor_civil_division "Minor civil division") (MCDs), which are in a separate category.[[2]](#cite_note-GARM_Chapter_9_-_Places-2)

The population and [demographics](/wiki/Demographics "Demographics") of the CDP are included in the data of county subdivisions containing the CDP. Generally, a CDP shall not be defined within the boundaries of what the Census Bureau regards to be an incorporated city, village or borough.[[2]](#cite_note-GARM_Chapter_9_-_Places-2) However, the Census Bureau considers some towns in New England states, [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey") and [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") as well as townships in some other states as MCDs, even though they are [incorporated municipalities](/wiki/Municipal_corporation "Municipal corporation") in those states. In such states, CDPs may be defined within such towns or spanning the boundaries of multiple towns.[[2]](#cite_note-GARM_Chapter_9_-_Places-2)

## Purpose

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census-designated_place&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Purpose")]

There are a number of reasons for the CDP designation:

* The area may be more urban than its surroundings, having a concentration of population with a definite residential nucleus, such as [Whitmore Lake, Michigan](/wiki/Whitmore_Lake,_Michigan "Whitmore Lake, Michigan"); [Hershey, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Hershey,_Pennsylvania "Hershey, Pennsylvania"); [Metairie, Louisiana](/wiki/Metairie,_Louisiana "Metairie, Louisiana"); and [The Villages, Florida](/wiki/The_Villages,_Florida "The Villages, Florida") (the latter CDP covering only a portion of the overall community).
* A formerly incorporated place may disincorporate or be partly [annexed](/wiki/Annexation "Annexation") by a neighboring town, but the former town or a part of it may still be reported by the census as a CDP by meeting criteria for a CDP. Examples are the former village of [Covedale (village in Ohio)](/wiki/Covedale,_Cincinnati,_Ohio "Covedale, Cincinnati, Ohio"), compared with [Covedale (CDP), Ohio](/wiki/Covedale_(CDP),_Ohio "Covedale (CDP), Ohio"), or the former village of [Seneca Falls (CDP), New York](/wiki/Seneca_Falls_(CDP),_New_York "Seneca Falls (CDP), New York"), disincorporated in 2011.
* The area may contain an easily recognizable institution, usually occupying a large land area, with an identity distinct from the surrounding community. This could apply to some college campuses and large [military bases](/wiki/Military_base "Military base") (or parts of a military base) that are not within the limits of any existing community, such as [Notre Dame, Indiana](/wiki/Notre_Dame,_Indiana "Notre Dame, Indiana"); [Stanford, California](/wiki/Stanford,_California "Stanford, California") (which houses the academic core of the [Stanford University](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University") campus);[[a]](#cite_note-10) [Fort Campbell North, Kentucky](/wiki/Fort_Campbell_North,_Kentucky "Fort Campbell North, Kentucky"); and [Fort Leonard Wood, Missouri](/wiki/Fort_Leonard_Wood,_Missouri "Fort Leonard Wood, Missouri").[[2]](#cite_note-GARM_Chapter_9_-_Places-2)
* In other cases, the boundary of an incorporated place may bisect a recognized community. An example of this is [Bostonia, California](/wiki/Bostonia,_California "Bostonia, California"), which straddles the city limits of [El Cajon](/wiki/El_Cajon,_California "El Cajon, California"). The [USGS](/wiki/United_States_Geological_Survey "United States Geological Survey") places the nucleus of Bostonia within El Cajon. The Bostonia CDP covers the greater El Cajon area in unincorporated [San Diego County](/wiki/San_Diego_County "San Diego County") that is generally north of that part of Bostonia within El Cajon.
* In some states, a CDP may be defined within an incorporated municipality that (for the purposes of the census) is regarded as a [minor civil division](/wiki/Minor_civil_division "Minor civil division"). For example, all [towns in New England](/wiki/New_England_town "New England town") are incorporated municipalities, but may also include both rural and urban areas. CDPs may be defined to describe urbanized areas within such municipalities, as in the case of [North Amherst, Massachusetts](/wiki/North_Amherst,_Massachusetts "North Amherst, Massachusetts").
* [Hawaii](/wiki/Hawaii "Hawaii") is the only state that has no incorporated places recognized by the U.S. Census Bureau below the county level. All data for places in Hawaii reported by the census are CDPs.[[2]](#cite_note-GARM_Chapter_9_-_Places-2)
* A few CDPs represent an aggregation of several nearby communities - for example, [Shorewood–Tower Hills–Harbert, Michigan](/wiki/Shorewood%E2%80%93Tower_Hills%E2%80%93Harbert,_Michigan "Shorewood–Tower Hills–Harbert, Michigan"), or [Egypt Lake-Leto, Florida](/wiki/Egypt_Lake-Leto,_Florida "Egypt Lake-Leto, Florida"). However, the Census Bureau discontinued this method for most CDPs during the [2010 census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census").[[6]](#cite_note-Census2010-6)
* In rare cases, a CDP was also defined for the urbanized area surrounding an incorporated municipality, but which is outside the municipal boundaries, for example, [Greater Galesburg, Michigan](/wiki/Greater_Galesburg,_Michigan "Greater Galesburg, Michigan"), or [Greater Upper Marlboro, Maryland](/wiki/Greater_Upper_Marlboro,_Maryland "Greater Upper Marlboro, Maryland"). This practice was discontinued in [2010](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census").[[6]](#cite_note-Census2010-6)
* In some states, the Census Bureau designates entire minor civil divisions (MCD) with an urban or suburban character as CDPs. Examples include [West Bloomfield Township, Michigan](/wiki/West_Bloomfield_Township,_Michigan "West Bloomfield Township, Michigan") (now listed as a [Charter township](/wiki/Charter_township "Charter township")) or [Reading, Massachusetts](/wiki/Reading,_Massachusetts "Reading, Massachusetts") (listed as a [New England town](/wiki/New_England_town "New England town") and as a CDP). Such designations were used in states where the MCDs function with strong governmental authority and provide services equivalent to an incorporated municipality (New England, New York, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Michigan, Minnesota, and Wisconsin). MCDs appear in a separate category in census data from places (i.e., incorporated places and CDPs); however, when MCDs strongly resemble incorporated places, CDPs coterminous with the MCDs are defined so that such places appear in both categories of census data.[[6]](#cite_note-Census2010-6)

## Criteria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Census-designated_place&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Criteria")]

Census Bureau criteria and guidelines specify that CDPs:[[4]](#cite_note-CDPCriteria-4)

* Constitute a single, named, contiguous geographic area containing a mix of residential, nonresidential, and commercial uses. Some predominantly residential communities may also be recognized as CDPs.
* Cannot be partially or entirely within an incorporated place or another CDP.
* Can be located in more than one county but cannot cross state boundaries.
* Contain at least some population or housing units. The Census Bureau may request a justification for CDPs delineated with fewer than ten housing units.
* May not have the same name as an adjacent or nearby incorporated place. Adding a directional to the name to differentiate is not acceptable if that name is not in local use.
* Can have a name change if the new name provides a better identification of the community.
* Boundaries should follow visible features such as roads, rivers, railroads, or nonvisible features such as parcel boundaries, adjacent incorporated place boundaries, or other Census Bureau geographies (e.g., school district boundaries, block group boundaries, etc.).
